title: Agitation and tremor two days after admission

chart.oneLiner: 55M, admitted 2 days ago after a fall, becomes acutely agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic, and confused with visual hallucinations.

opening: Get them off me — there are bugs everywhere! Why am I here?
patientFile:
PERSONA: 55-year-old man, agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic, disoriented, picking at the sheets, frightened by visual hallucinations. Poor historian.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks family/nursing/chart/medication list): Admitted 2 days ago after a mechanical fall. Family reports he drinks heavily every day (a pint of liquor daily) and his last drink was ~2 days ago, around admission. He had mild tremor and anxiety yesterday that has now escalated. No prior withdrawal seizures known. No new infection identified yet but he is febrile. MEDS: none regular; received acetaminophen for pain.

HPI (from patient — limited): agitated, not oriented, reports seeing bugs/insects.
PMH: hypertension, prior alcohol use. SOCIAL: heavy daily alcohol use.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: agitated, diaphoretic, coarse tremor, hypervigilant.
- Vitals: BP 168/98, HR 122, RR 22, 38.1°C, SpO2 97% RA (autonomic hyperactivity).
- Neuro: disoriented, tremulous, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
- No obvious new head trauma since admission; pupils equal/reactive.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- The patient is agitated/confused: give disjointed, hallucination-tinged answers. Provide COLLATERAL (heavy daily drinking, last drink ~2 days ago, admission timeline) only when the student explicitly asks family/nursing/chart/what happened.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BMP: Na 137, K 3.2 (low), Mg 1.3 (low), Phos 1.8 (low), Cr 0.9 — typical electrolyte derangements.
labs.Infection_workup: Cultures/UA/CXR to exclude infection driving the picture — no clear source found.
labs.CT_head: If focal deficit or unclear: CT head — no acute intracranial process.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia ~180 with no clearly visible P waves (retrograde P possible).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia ~180 with no clearly visible P waves (retrograde P possible). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential for this acute agitation/confusion (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens (leading,7). Infection/sepsis (CANT-MISS,4). Hypoglycemia (CANT-MISS,3). Intracranial process — subdural after the fall (CANT-MISS,4). Wernicke encephalopathy (core,3). Other drug withdrawal/intoxication or electrolyte derangement (core,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — heavy drinking, last drink ~2 days ago, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Infection/sepsis; Hypoglycemia; Intracranial process — subdural after the fall. Also reasonable: Wernicke encephalopathy; Other drug withdrawal/intoxication or electrolyte derangement.

criticalActions.item: Intracranial process — subdural after the fall

coreActions.item: Other drug withdrawal/intoxication or electrolyte derangement
Named leading dx w/ justification — heavy drinking, last drink ~2 days ago, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations

mustNotMiss: Intracranial process — subdural after the fall
acceptableAlternatives: Other drug withdrawal/intoxication or electrolyte derangement

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick glucose (CANT-MISS,3). BMP incl. magnesium, potassium, phosphate (CANT-MISS,3). CBC (2). LFTs (1). Infection screen — cultures/UA/CXR (2). CT head given the recent fall + confusion (CANT-MISS,3). Formal withdrawal scoring (CIWA-Ar) (1). PENALTY: anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection or intracranial bleed (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Fingerstick glucose; magnesium, potassium, phosphate; CT head given the recent fall + confusion.

criticalActions.item: CT head given the recent fall + confusion

penalties.item: anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection or intracranial bleed

requiredTests: CT head given the recent fall + confusion
contraindicatedTests: anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection or intracranial bleed
orderSensitiveActions: anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection or intracranial bleed

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia; consider QT with low Mg/K; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (6); comments on checking QT given electrolyte derangements (4); no acute ischemia (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: no clearly identifiable sinus P waves
commonMistakes: not noting the absence of preceding P waves
steps.idealAnswer: Paroxysmal SVT: regular narrow-complex tachycardia at ~180 without visible sinus P waves.

coreActions: no clearly identifiable sinus P waves

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear or possible aspiration. CREDIT: identifies a clear/near-clear film (6); notes it screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration
criticalFindings: Clear or possible aspiration; obtained to exclude an infectious precipitant.
steps.idealAnswer: Clear or possible aspiration; obtained to exclude an infectious precipitant.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Clear or possible aspiration; obtained to exclude an infectious precipitant.

coreActions.item: notes it screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: With a normal glucose, no intracranial bleed, and this presentation, what is your diagnosis?
steps.rubric: Delirium tremens / severe alcohol withdrawal (6). Cites autonomic hyperactivity + hallucinations + tremor + timing from last drink (3). Notes DTs is a medical emergency with significant mortality (bonus,1).

bonusActions.item: Notes DTs is a medical emergency with significant mortality

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Benzodiazepines, ideally symptom-triggered by CIWA (CANT-MISS,8). Thiamine BEFORE/with glucose to prevent Wernicke (CANT-MISS,5). Replete magnesium, potassium, phosphate (4). Supportive care in a monitored/ICU setting; treat fever/infection (4). Reassess frequently; reserve antipsychotics only as adjunct (they lower seizure threshold) (2). PENALTY: giving glucose before thiamine (-3); antipsychotic monotherapy or inadequate benzodiazepines (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Plan: Benzodiazepines, ideally symptom-triggered by CIWA; Thiamine BEFORE/with glucose to prevent Wernicke.

criticalActions.item: Thiamine BEFORE/with glucose to prevent Wernicke

requiredActions: Thiamine BEFORE/with glucose to prevent Wernicke

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 55M, admitted 2 days ago after a fall, becomes acutely agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic, and confused with visual hallucinations. The diagnosis is Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neuro: disoriented, tremulous, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
Neuro: disoriented, tremulous, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
General: agitated, diaphoretic, coarse tremor, hypervigilant.
Neuro: disoriented, tremulous, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
